subjectLine: Spiny mice crack the inflammaging code 🧬
borisTake: Clusterin-elevated macrophages keeping the thymus functional past 4 years in outbred mice is the upstream inflammaging brake I've been waiting to see identified — it's working proximal to the SASP loop rather than cleaning up downstream like our current senolytic protocols. This is where a small molecule screen should be running right now.
leadAnalysis: The golden spiny mouse's exceptional aging resistance maps directly to macrophage behavior: elevated clusterin expression restrains the inflammatory secretome, suppressing senescent cell accumulation and fibrosis before they cascade. The standout finding is preserved thymic architecture past 4 years of age, maintaining naïve T cell output at a time when standard lab mice show complete thymic involution — functional immune aging running in reverse. Critically, these were outbred animals in non-pathogen-free conditions, meaning the biology holds in ecologically realistic environments rather than sterile lab chambers. The translational target is clear: pharmacological induction of macrophage clusterin — via AAV, small molecules, or clusterin-mimetic peptides — could become a single intervention that simultaneously addresses inflammaging, thymic decay, and downstream senescent cell clearance.
oneNumber: 4 years | Age at which golden spiny mice maintain full thymic architecture and naïve T cell output — standard lab mice show complete involution well before this

papers.title: Immunometabolic Resistors of Aging in Long-Lived Golden Spiny Mice
papers.summary: Golden spiny mice (A. russatus) resist age-related functional decline in non-SPF conditions, maintaining thymic architecture past 4 years. Elevated macrophage clusterin restrains inflammaging and preserves naïve T cell output — identifying a pharmacologically targetable brake on immune aging.

papers.title: Partial Reprogramming Is Conserved from Insect to Mammal
papers.summary: OSKM-driven partial reprogramming extends lifespan in both Drosophila and mice, with crossover implications for designing conserved reprogramming protocols in mammals. Combined with senolytics and aging clocks, the authors argue this constitutes a near-complete therapeutic toolkit for aging intervention.

papers.title: Multidomain Lifestyle Intervention Improves Epigenetic Aging in Frail Older Adults: RCT
papers.summary: RCT in frail older adults: combined exercise, nutrition, cognitive, and social intervention improved functional trajectories AND reduced epigenetic age on validated clocks. One of the cleaner demonstrations that non-pharmacological multi-domain protocols move biological age in a clinically relevant population.

papers.title: Epigenetic Age Deceleration Reflects Exercise-Induced Cardiorespiratory Fitness Improvements
papers.summary: VO2max gains from structured training correlate with measurable epigenetic age deceleration across multiple clock algorithms. Links fitness adaptation — not just activity volume — to biological age, adding mechanistic credibility to exercise as a first-line geroprotector.

papers.title: Glycolytic ATP Decline as an Evolved Mechanism of Aging
papers.summary: Proposes that the age-related shift from fast glycolytic to slower oxidative ATP production is an evolved resource-allocation mechanism benefiting offspring at the cost of parental longevity. Naked mole rats' sustained glycolytic flux under hypoxia supports the model — with drug target implications.

papers.title: INSITE: Injectable Self-Assembled Hepatocyte Platform for Ectopic Liver Tissue
papers.summary: Ultrasound-guided injection of primary hepatocytes in hydrogel microspheres self-assembles into vascularised pseudo-liver tissue at ectopic sites. Long-term hepatocyte function confirmed in vivo — minimally invasive alternative to transplant with direct relevance to age-related liver failure.

papers.title: Pitavastatin Slows Epigenetic Aging in HIV: REPRIEVE Substudy
papers.summary: Pitavastatin — proven to cut cardiovascular events in HIV — also decelerates epigenetic aging in this REPRIEVE pilot. First statin-class evidence linking lipid-lowering therapy to clock-measured biological age, opening a repurposing angle for broader aging populations.
